coredata.dc:title: Colloidal carbon quantum dots as light absorber for efficient and stable ecofriendly photoelectrochemical hydrogen generation
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Xiaohan
Wang, Maorong
Liu, Guiju
Zhang, Yuanming
Han, Guangting
Vomiero, Alberto
Zhao, Haiguang
coredata.dc:description: Solar-driven hydrogen production is one of the most promising strategies for solar-to-hydrogen energy conversion. Compared to inorganic quantum dots (QDs), carbon quantum dots (C-dots) have attracted a lot of attention for optoelectronic devices due to their structure-dependent optical properties and green composition. However, the solar-to-hydrogen conversion efficiency of most of the photoelectrochemical (PEC) devices based on colloidal QDs is still low. Here we demonstrated a highly efficient and stable ecofriendly PEC device using C-dots sensitized TiO2 photoanode, Pt loaded on carbon nanofibers as counter electrode, and glucose aqueous solution as electrolyte. The red-color C-dots were prepared using a solvothermal reaction, with an absorption spectrum ranging from 300 to 600 nm and a quantum yield (QY) of 50%. The C-dots have excitation independent photoluminescence peak positions and highly crystalline structure. The hydroxyl group on the C-dots can strongly interact with the TiO2, forming a very stable complex. Benefiting from these features, the PEC devices based on C-dots exhibit a saturated photocurrent density as high as ~4 mA/cm2 at 0.6 V vs. RHE and the device is very stable (keeping 95% of its initial value after 10-hour illumination upon 100 mW/cm2). This work indicates the promising properties of the C-dots/TiO2 system, which holds huge potential for applications in the fields of optoelectronic and catalytic devices.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Representative BF TEM (a) HRTEM (b) images of Y C-dots, (c) HRTEM image of Y C-dots/TiO2 composite. BF TEM (d) and HRTEM (e) images of R C-dots, (f) HRTEM image of R C-dots/TiO2 composite.

Absorption (a) and PL (b) spectra of R C-dots dispersed in ethanol solution. The excitation wavelength is λ ex= 350–450 nm. Inset of (b) is the Abs and PL spectra of R C-dots. (c) Time-resolved PL spectra of the R C-dots with emission at 525 and 550 nm, respectively. The excitation wavelength is fixed at 370 nm. Absorption (d) and PL (e) spectra of Y C-dots dispersed in ethanol solution. The excitation wavelength is λ ex= 350–450 nm. Inset of (b) is the Abs and PL spectra of Y C-dots. (f) Proposed energy diagram and energy transition in C-dots.

High resolution XPS of: (a) Ti 2p, and (b) O 1s. Inset of (a) is the diagram of the interaction between the C-dots capped with hydroxyl group and TiO2. The gray, cyan, red and blue dots indicate O, H, C and Ti atom, respectively.

(a) Schematic diagram of a typical PEC device using C-dots/TiO2 as anode, Pt/CNFs as counter electrode, Ag/AgCl as reference electrode, and glucose aqueous solution as electrolyte. (b) Schematic diagram and approximate band alignment of C-dots/TiO2 sensitized PEC cell. The energy levels of the C-dots and glucose was obtained from CV measurement. (c) A typical SEM image of CNFs. (d) Represented TEM image of Pt loaded CNFs. Inset of (d) is the HRTEM of a single Pt nanoparticle. (e) J-V curve of counter electrode in the glucose aqueous solution.

(a) J-V (versus RHE) of yellow and red C-dots/TiO2-sensitized photoelectrodes in the chopped illumination (100 mW/cm2). The electrolyte contained 0.2 M glucose and 0.2 M Na2SO4 in aqueous solution; (b-c) Optimized PEC device. (b) J-V (versus RHE) of optimized PEC device based on R C-dots/TiO2 in the dark, under continuous and chopped illumination (100 mW/cm2). (c) Photocurrent density versus illumination time at 0.6 V versus RHE under illumination (100 mW/cm2) for the photoanodes based on R C-dots/TiO2. The anode was prepared with double TiO2 layer, and the electrolyte contained 0.4 M glucose and 0.2 M Na2SO4 in aqueous solution. The incubation time for C-dots was optimized as 3 h with 5 mg/mL C-dots dispersed in menthol.

Performance and structure of PEC devices based on different types of nanocrystals under illumination (AM 1.5 G, 100 mW/cm2). For fairly comparison of the reported performance, the different types of electrolytes were included in the table.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Solar-driven hydrogen production is one of the most promising strategies for solar-to-hydrogen energy conversion. Compared to inorganic quantum dots (QDs), carbon quantum dots (C-dots) have attracted a lot of attention for optoelectronic devices due to their structure-dependent optical properties and green composition. However, the solar-to-hydrogen conversion efficiency of most of the photoelectrochemical (PEC) devices based on colloidal QDs is still low. Here we demonstrated a highly efficient and stable ecofriendly PEC device using C-dots sensitized TiO2 photoanode, Pt loaded on carbon nanofibers as counter electrode, and glucose aqueous solution as electrolyte. The red-color C-dots were prepared using a solvothermal reaction, with an absorption spectrum ranging from 300 to 600 nm and a quantum yield (QY) of 50%. The C-dots have excitation independent photoluminescence peak positions and highly crystalline structure. The hydroxyl group on the C-dots can strongly interact with the TiO2, forming a very stable complex. Benefiting from these features, the PEC devices based on C-dots exhibit a saturated photocurrent density as high as ~4 mA/cm2 at 0.6 V vs. RHE and the device is very stable (keeping 95% of its initial value after 10-hour illumination upon 100 mW/cm2). This work indicates the promising properties of the C-dots/TiO2 system, which holds huge potential for applications in the fields of optoelectronic and catalytic devices.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid growth of global population, the increasing use of energy through fossil fuels has caused severe environmental pollution and climate change due to the over release of carbon emission [1–4]. It is highly important to produce clean energy using sustainable natural resources [1,5–16]. Directly converting solar light into hydrogen through photoelectrochemical (PEC) or photochemical devices has attracted a lot of attention because hydrogen is a clean and sustainable fuel with high energy density [17–20]. Up to now, many types of semiconductors have been used as efficient absorbers for optoelectronic devices [2–4,17–32]. Typically, in a PEC system, semiconductors absorb sunlight and convert it into electron-hole pairs; subsequently, the electron-hole pair separates and migrates to the corresponding electrodes for water reduction or oxidation reaction [2]. As electron-hole pair can easily recombine or be quenched because of the surface defects/traps on semiconductors, the solar-to-hydrogen conversion efficiency is still low in most semiconductor-based PEC devices [2].

Among various types of semiconductors, quantum dots (QDs) have been used as a new type of absorbers for efficient hydrogen production due to their size-dependent excellent optical properties, such as broad absorption, high absorption coefficient, high quantum yield (QY) and size-tunable band energy levels. Inorganic QDs have been widely studied for PEC devices [17,18,33]. For example, by optimizing the core/shell structure in CdSe/CdS QDs, the saturated current density (J) in PEC devices based on QDs/TiO2 increases up 10–17 mA/cm2 [18,34,35]. However, there are still several serious issues present in the inorganic QDs based PEC devices: (i) most of used QDs (PbS, CdSe/CdS, CdS, CuInSe, ZnAgInSx, Ag2S) contain either toxic elements (Cd, Pb) or expensive non-earth-abundant elements (e.g. Ag, In) [33,36–41]. Compared to widely used metal oxides (e.g. TiO2, and ZnO) [42–45], these elements prohibit these types of QDs for practical use in PEC hydrogen production; (ii) the electrolyte used in most of the PEC devices contains chemically-active Na2S/Na2SO3, which serves as efficient hole scavenger [17,19,20]. However, this type of electrolyte (pH of 13) is very corrosive and may release H2S during chemical storage and handling. In addition, the QDs become unstable during cell operation in such electrolyte due to both the chemical- and photo- corrosion [17,19,20]; (iii) Pt plate/wire or commercial Pt/C is very efficient for water reduction, but Pt is very expensive and not an earth-abundant element. Even though the transition metal oxides/sulfides are found to be efficient for PEC devices as counter electrode, their long-term stability is still a challenge compared to Pt [25]. It is highly desirable to produce efficient and stable PEC devices based on environmentally-friendly and cost-effective materials or reducing the used amount of Pt by increasing the active surface of Pt material.

Recently, carbon quantum dots (C-dots) have been used for optoelectronic devices, such as solar cells, luminescent solar concentrators, photoredox catalysis, battery and bio-sensors due to their excellent optical and electrical properties [1,8,46–48]. The C-dots contain earth-abundant elements, such as C, N and O; they can be easily prepared by using wet-chemistry approaches (microwave reaction, vacuum-heating and hydro/solvo-thermal reaction) using earth-abundant and renewable precursors, such as citric acid and glucose [8,49–53]. The absorption of C-dots covers from 300 to 900 nm and can be controlled by their size, composition and surface functional groups [8,49–53]. The band energy levels of C-dots can be adjusted to be suitable for the water splitting. Last but not least, the colloidal stability and photostability of C-dots are superior compared to inorganic QDs (e.g. Cd/Pb based QDs, In/Ag based QDs, Sn based QDs and perovskite QDs) [8,43,44,49,50,54–62]. Recently, C-dots have been used as light absorber for hydrogen production by sensitizing metal oxides [42,43,45,50,52,55,56,62,63]. Benefiting from either the enhancement of the sunlight absorption or improved charge separation/transport, the solar-to-hydrogen conversion efficiency of the modified devices can be dramatically enhanced. For example, Ye et al. reported that C-dots could enhance the sunlight absorption of the NiOOH/FeOOH/C-dots/BiVO4 photoanode, achieving a high J of 3.91 and 5.99 mA/cm2 at 0.6 V and 1.23 V vs. reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) under AM 1.5 G in KH2PO4 aqueous solution [8]. Li et al. reported C-dots decorated Cu2S nanowire array-based PEC device with a J of 1.05 mA/cm2 at 0 V vs. RHE under AM 1.5 G in 1.0 M KCl aqueous solution [64]. Zhou et al. used the C-dots modified anatase/rutile TiO2 nanorods photoanode, generating a J of 2.76 mA/cm2 at 1.23 V vs. RHE under AM 1.5 G in 0.2 M Na2SO4 and 1 M Na2SO3 aqueous electrolyte [44]. Although C-dots based PEC devices have shown a great potential for hydrogen production, the presently obtained current density J is still lower than other PEC devices based on Cd/Pb QDs, which have a typical J between 10 and 20 mA/cm2 [18,35]. It is still a challenge to obtain highly efficient PEC devices using colloidal C-dots. Considering the reported C-dots currently used for PEC devices, (i) the C-dots have a limited absorption, mainly in the ultraviolet range (300–450 nm), which matches only a small portion of Sun’s spectrum; (ii) the C-dots have excitation-dependent photoluminescent properties, making the band energy levels of C-dots tunable, leading to slow charge injection; (iii) the C-dots do not have a well-defined crystalline structure, holding molecular-like structure inside or on the surface of C-dots. This behavior usually leads to slow charge mobility.

In order to solve the above-mentioned problems, here we demonstrated a highly efficient and stable PEC device using red C-dots sensitized TiO2 as photoanode, glucose aqueous solution (pH = 7) as electrolyte and Pt loaded on carbon nanofibers (Pt/CNFs) with low Pt loading amount as counter electrode. The C-dots were prepared using a solvothermal reaction, with the absorption spectrum ranging from 300 to 600 nm and a quantum yield (QY) of 50%. The C-dots have excitation independent emission peak positions and a highly crystalline structure with well-defined energy levels, which result in discrete de-excitation spectra, avoiding the quasi-continuum energy spectrum, which is typical of the surface/defect states. The hydroxyl group on the C-dots can strongly interact with the TiO2, forming a very stable complex compared to carboxyl capped C-dots. Benefiting from these features, the PEC devices based on red C-dots using glucose solution as electrolyte exhibit a saturated J as high as ~4 mA/cm2 and an outstanding stability. The J of the device can maintain 95% of its initial value after 10 h illumination (100 mW/cm2). Furthermore, the Pt plate can be replaced by Pt/CNFs with Pt loading ten times lower than commercial Pt/C catalysts. This work indicates that the red C-dots/TiO2-Glucose-Pt/CNFs system potentially applies to the fields of ecofriendly optoelectronic and catalytic devices.
Phloroglucinol, sulfuric acid, methanol, glucose, Na2SO4, ethanol, and dimethyl formamide (DMF), dichloromethane (99.9%) were purchased from Aladdin. All chemicals were used as-received.

C-QDs were prepared following the reported reference using a hydrothermal approach. [64] Typically, five hundred milligrams phloroglucinol was mixed with 10 mL of DMF with 2 mL of concentrated H2SO4 as catalyst. The solution was heated at 200 °C for 3 or 12 h, then the autoclave was naturally cooled down to 25 °C. The C-dots mixture was further purified using silica column chromatography, and details were included in the S.I. Subsequently, the mixture was transferred into dialysis bags (molecular weight of 500 Da) for 12 h. The C-dots/methanol solution inside the dialysis bag was collected for further characterizations and use in anode fabrication. C-dots capped with carboxyl group were prepared by following the reported method and details are included in the S.I. [66].

A single layer or double layer TiO2 mesoporous film was prepared using a drop-casting approach. [20] Details are included in the S.I. The TiO2 film was incubated in C-dots methanol solution for 3–10 h. Then the unattached C-dots were washed away by using methanol or ethanol solution for 3 times. Then the C-dots/ TiO2 was dried naturally and kept at 120 °C for 20 min in air.

The Pt/CNFs were prepared using atomic layer deposited (ALD) onto a CNFs derived from lignin. Details are included in the S.I. Through the electrospinning, pyrolysis and washing procedure, the as-spun nanofibers are converted into the CNFs films [26]. Then the prepared CNFs films were put into the ALD chamber to obtain the Pt nanocluster on the surface of the CNFs.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) of C-dots and C-dots/TiO2 were performed using a JEOL 2100F TEM. X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) was performed in an AXIS SUPRA equipped with a hemispherical analyzer. Absorption spectra were acquired with a UV-2600 UV-Vis spectrophotometer. Steady state photoluminescence (PL) characterization of the C-dots was performed on an Edinburgh FLS980 instrument. The H NMR was performed using a BRUKER AVANCEⅢHD 400 MHz. The FT-IR spectra were acquired with a NICOLET iS 5. The content of Pt in the CNFs was measured by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-OES, Agilent 730). The loading amount of Pt was 0.0034 mg/cm2 (details shown in Table S1).

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements: A three-electrode cell configuration was used for CV measurement using an electrochemical workstation (CHI 760E). Platinum was used as counter electrode; an Ag/AgCl was used as reference electrode and materials coated glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was used as working electrode. Ferrocene/ferrocenium (Fc/Fc+) dispersed 0.1 M tetrabutyl ammonium perchlorate (TBAP) was used as internal standard with a scan rate of 50 mV/s [67,68]. For the preparation of working electrode, typically the C-dots or glucose was mixed with Nafion and sonicated for 10 min. Subsequently, the mixture was dropped on the GCE and died at 25 °C for 12 h before testing. The energy levels of the C-dots were calculated based on Refs. [67,68].

PEC measurements: The performance of the C-dots/TiO2 anode was measured by using a three-electrode configuration using an electrochemical workstation (CHI 760E). The J-V curves were measured using an Oriel LCS-100 solar simulator (AM 1.5 G, 100 mW/cm2). The working area of the photoanode was ~0.15 cm2. All the PEC measurements were carried out by illuminating the sunlight on the back side of photoanode (Glass/FTO/TiO2/C-dots).

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements: EIS was performed in the frequency range of 105 to 10−2 Hz with an amplitude of 5 mV at the open circuit potential under solar simulator (AM 1.5 G, 100 mW/cm2) irradiation. The Nyquist plots were fitted using ZSimpWin software.

Electrocatalytic Hydrogen Evolution Reaction (HER) Measurements: All HER measurements were performed by using a three-electrode configuration using an electrochemical workstation (CHI 760E) with a three-electrode setup. A platinum (Pt) or Pt/CNFs, a Pt and Ag/AgCl electrode were used as working, counter and reference electrode, respectively. Glucose (0.4 M) and Na2SO4 (0.2 M) that degassed by bubbling N2 for 15 min were used as the electrolyte. Linear scanning voltammetry (LSV) was carried out at a scan rate of 5 mV/s.

The measured potentials vs. Ag/AgCl reference electrode were converted to the RHE according to the Nernst equation (E RHE = E Ag/AgCl + 0.059 × pH + 0.1976), where EAg/AgCl is the applied potential vs. Ag/AgCl and 0.1976 V is the standard potential of the Ag/AgCl reference electrode at 25 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and optical properties of C-dots
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to obtain C-dots with a highly crystalline structure and broad absorption, we synthesized C-dots via a solvothermal approach using phloroglucinol as precursor [64]. With all other identical reaction parameters, the yellow and red color C-dots were obtained by heating the reaction for 3 h and 12 h, respectively (Fig. S1a). Fig. 1 shows the typical transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of yellow and red C-dots and their corresponding C-dots/TiO2 composites. It can be seen from the bright-field (BF) TEM images that both the two kinds of C-dots exhibit quasi-spherical shape (Fig. 1a and d). The C-dots with red color (R C-dots) have a typical mean size of ~ 4.1 nm (Fig. 1a, d, Fig. S2). Benefiting from the structure of phloroglucinol, the as-produced C-dots have a highly crystalline structure. Fig. 1(b) is the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of an individual yellow C-dots (Y C-dots). The lattice spacing of 2.04 Å corresponds to the (101) plane of graphite carbon (Fig. 1b) [65]. Fig. 1(e) is the HRTEM image of an individual R C-dots. The lattice spacings of the two planes with an angle of 56° are measured to be 3.33 Å, corresponding to the (202) and (20
2
̅
) planes of hexagonal carbon, respectively. The surface of the as-synthesized C-dots was capped with hydroxyl (-OH) group (3380 cm−1), which was confirmed by the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) measurement (Fig. S3) [65].

The C-dots have an absorption spectrum ranging from 300 to 600 nm. By varying the reaction time, we could obtain the C-dots with different absorption spectra. The C-dots have a typical PL spectrum with two peaks, which have been found in the literature. [65] For example, the R C-dots have emissions located at 526 nm and 548 nm, respectively ( Fig. 2a). With different excitation wavelengths, the peak positions do not show any shift (Fig. 2b). To gain more insight into the exciton recombination dynamics, time-resolved PL spectra were measured for R C-dots with emission peaks located at 525 nm and 550 nm, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2(c), very slight average lifetime variation was found ((3.7 ± 0.2) ns at 550 nm versus (4.0 ± 0.2) ns at 525 nm) for R C-dots with an excitation wavelength of 370 nm. This result together with the excitation-independent emission indicates that the two peaks originate from the bandgap emission, other than surface-dominated emission (Fig. 2f). [65] The C-dots have a bright yellow or red color upon UV illumination (Fig. S1b). The obtained QY of Y C-dots in this work is around (40 ± 5) % and the R C-dots have the QY of (50 ± 5) % in ethanol solution. The broad absorption of C-dots matches well the Sun’s spectrum in the UV and visible range and may be suitable as an efficient sunlight collector for PEC hydrogen production. In addition, the high QY in the as-prepared C-dots endow the less charge recombination because of the trap-centers.

The as-prepared C-dots were dispersed in methanol and then mesoporous TiO2 thin film was dipped into the above solution for C-dots adsorption. After 3–10 h incubation, the light yellow or red C-dots/TiO2 film can be obtained, as shown in Fig. S1c and Fig. S4. The C-dots capped with hydroxyl group can attach very well on the TiO2 film. Fig. 1(c) shows the HRTEM image of the Y C-dots/TiO2 composite. The two different lattice structures at the edge and middle regions in the HRTEM image are clearly visible (Fig. 1c). The lattice spacing of 3.51 Å in the left region corresponds to the anatase TiO2. The particle indicated by the dotted line in the middle region is the C-dots. Fig. 1(f) exhibits the combination of R C-dots and the anatase TiO2. The lattice spacing of 2.41 Å corresponds to the (213) plane of hexagonal carbon, and the lattice spacing of 3.51 Å corresponds to the (101) plane of TiO2, which indicates that the C-dots are attached to the surface of the TiO2. From Fig. 1(c) and (f), one can conclude that both the Y C-dots and R C-dots can be well deposited in the TiO2 porous films. In order to further verify the attachment mechanism of the C-dots on the TiO2 particles, we carried on X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) measurement. The C-dots/TiO2 film contains C, O and Ti elements, with C/O/Ti molar ratio of 60:16:1 (Fig. S5). The molar ratio of O/Ti in C-dots/TiO2 film is five times higher than that of TiO2 film, indicating that the surface of TiO2 was mainly covered by the C-dots as the XPS mainly detects the signal from the surface of sample. Fig. 3(b) presents the O 1s spectra of C-dots, TiO2 and C-dots/TiO2. The C-dots have O 1s peak located at 532.3 eV, originating from the C‒OH bond. The TiO2 sample has two peaks located at 529.49 eV and 531.01 eV, assigned to Ti-O and Ti-OH. As expected, the O 1s spectrum of C-dots/TiO2 can be fitted with three peaks at 529.43 eV, 531.35 eV and 532.54 eV [66]. The molar ratio of Ti-OH/Ti-O is 3/3 in C-dots/TiO2, which is higher than 3/7 in TiO2. Meanwhile, the O 1s spectrum of C-dots/TiO2 has a 0.34 eV blue-shift for the peak position of 531.35 eV peak, compared to TiO2. Consistently compared to TiO2, Ti 2p peaks of C-dots/TiO2, exhibit a 0.25 eV red-shift. Both the increase in molar ratio of Ti-OH and the shift of Ti-O peaks indicate the formation of hydrogen bond between the TiO2 and C-dots (inset of Fig. 3a). The surface of TiO2 usually has the Ti-OH group, which can interact with the OH group of the C-dots. This also explains that the C-dots with hydroxyl group can attach stably on the TiO2, better than the carboxyl group on the surface of C-dots. For comparison, we also synthesized C-dots capped with carboxyl group (COOH) by heating the citric acid and urea at 160 °C for 3 h. [66] The as-prepared C-dots have a very narrow size distribution, with a typical absorption and emission spectra in the visible range (Fig. S6a–b). The C-dots were capped dominantly with carboxyl group, as confirmed by FT-IR measurement (Fig. S6d), which cannot form hydrogen bond with TiO2 under identical preparation procedure. The C-dots capped with carboxyl group can be easily washed off from the film, as indicated by the fading of the Green color of the C-dots/TiO2 film.

A typical three-electrode configuration was used for PEC hydrogen generation as shown in Fig. 4(a). Typically, C-dots/TiO2 film on the FTO glass was used as photoanode, and Pt/CNFs were used as counter electrode. The detailed fabrication process was included in the S.I. The thickness of the photoanode (C-dots/TiO2/FTO) is estimated to be around 12.6 µm based on the cross-sectional SEM image (Fig. S14). As shown in Fig. 4(c), the as-fabricated electrospun CNFs derived from lignin have a typical diameter of 300 nm. [26] After depositing Pt on the CNFs by atomic layer deposition, we can clearly observe the presence of Pt nanocluster on the surface or the cross-section of the CNFs. The lattice spacing of 2.25 Å corresponds to the (111) plane of Pt (Fig. 4d). The Pt/CNFs exhibit a comparable catalytic performance in the glucose aqueous solution compared to Pt plate and Pt-carbon/Glassy carbon electrode (Pt-C/GCE) (Fig. 4e). With respect to commercial Pt-C catalyst, in this work, the loading amount (mg/cm2) of Pt is 10 times lower as shown in Table S2, largely reducing the price of the counter electrode in C-dots based PEC device.

The electrolyte was an Na2SO4 aqueous solution with different concentrations of glucose with a typical pH of 6.8–6.9, which serves as a sacrificial hole scavenger. [62] We studied the band energy alignment for the above materials by using Cyclic voltammetry (CV). Ferrocene (Fc) was used as an internal standard for all the CV measurements. [67,68] As shown in Fig. S7a–c, the oxidation and reduction peaks of the glucose and C-dots were labeled as black and green asterisk, respectively. Following the approach reported from references, [67,68] we calculated the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy positions of the C-dots. EHOMO and ELUMO of the energy band edges for the C-dots were presented in Fig. 4(b). The calculated bandgap (R C-dots: 2.27 eV and Y C-dots: 2.61 eV) was consistent with the values derived from the dominant absorption peak of C-dots (R C-dots: 2.32 eV and Y C-dots: 2.57 eV). The energy levels of C-dots are suitable for water reduction or oxidation. We also carried out CV measurement to identify the oxidation and reduction peak of the glucose and the oxidation energy level was shown in Fig. S7a. As the measured glucose oxidation energy level is higher than that of water oxidation, in our PEC configuration, only hydrogen could be obtained, other than hydrogen and oxygen.

For the PEC devices, we used one-layer TiO2 film and fixed the concentration of C-dots (3 mg/mL), incubation time (3 h) and post-annealing temperature and time (100 °C, 30 min). A very small photoresponse (J less than 0.5 mA/cm2) was found in both TiO2 and R C-dots/TiO2 anodes using 0.2 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution as electrolyte (Fig. S8). With the addition of 0.2 M glucose, the J of C-dots /TiO2 anode increases to 0.8 mA/cm2 and further to 2.2 mA/cm2 by annealing the C-dots /TiO2 thin film at 100 °C for 30 min (Fig. S8). The higher J in annealed C-dots/TiO2 PEC device indicates the improved surface passivation of C-dots or the improved interaction between the C-dots and TiO2. Both of these effects could improve the charge transfer between the C-dots and TiO2, thus increasing J. Compared to Na2SO4 electrolyte, the mixed electrolyte including Na2SO4/glucose can largely improve the performance of the PEC device. In order to understand this mechanism, we carried on the FT-IR and NMR measurements for glucose after 10 h PEC measurement. We found the peak intensity of aldehyde group compared to that of C-O group decreases significantly as shown in Fig. S9, indicating that the glucose was oxidized by the holes. This conclusion was further confirmed by the H NMR (Fig. S10), in which a (COO)H group was found, compared to the reference spectra (glucose and glucose/Na2SO4). These results indicate that the glucose can serve as an efficient hole scavenger, thus reducing the e-h+ recombination rate [7,62]. As the glucose can be easily obtained from sustainable biomass, the use of glucose as hole scavenger not only improves the efficiency of the PEC device, but also makes the PEC system lower in cost and greener, compared to Na2S/Na2SO3 electrolyte.

By applying identical parameters to prepare the anode (single layer TiO2, 0.2 M glucose/0.2 M Na2SO4, 3 h incubation time in 3 mg/mL C-dots solution), the R C-dots based anode has the highest J compared to anodes based on pure TiO2, Y C-dots and COOH capped C-dots ( Fig. 5a and Fig. S6b). We found that the J of PEC devices is strongly dependent on the concentration of glucose, by keeping constant all the other parameters (Fig. S11). By carefully optimizing the structure and the light direction of the anode, including the use of bilayer TiO2 thin film, the concentration of glucose, the backside/front illumination of photoanode and the concentration of C-dots in methanol, the optimized PEC device exhibits the highest J value up to 4 mA/cm2 (Fig. 5b), which is comparable to the PEC devices reported in the literature, such as SnSe nanocrystals (8.5 mA/cm2 at 0.9 V vs. RHE) [20], NiOOH/FeOOH/C-dots/BiVO4 (3.91 and 5.99 mA/cm2 at 0.6 V and 1.23 V vs. RHE) [8]. Furthermore, the photocurrent densities under back illumination (FTO/TiO2/C-dots) was ~ 20% higher than that of the front illumination, consistently with the reported result [69]. Among the PEC devices based on C-dots, at lower potential (0.6 V), our obtained J is among the best ( Table 1), and in the potential range of 0.4–1.4 V, there is no significant change for J. However, for other PEC devices based on C-dots, the J-V response exhibit a typical potential-dependent J. For example, with the increase of the potential (from 0.2 to 1.23 V), the J of NiOOH/FeOOH/C-dots/BiVO4 gradually increases from 0 to 5.99 mA/cm2 [8]. As summarized in Table 1, the J of R C-dots/TiO2 (4 mA/cm2 at 0.6 V vs. RHE) is higher than those of other C-dots-based photoanodes, such as NiOOH/FeOOH/C-dots/BiVO4 (~ 3.91 mA/cm2 at 0.6 V vs. RHE) [8], C-dots modified anatase/rutile TiO2 nanorods (0.88 mA/cm2 at 0.6 V vs. RHE) [44], C3N4 QDs/ZnO (0.952 mA/cm2 at 0.6 V vs. RHE) [43], C-dots/ZnO (0.83 mA/cm2 at 0.6 V vs. RHE) [42], Compared to above work, although in current work, glucose was used as hole scavengers, it is still important for the potential application of the PEC hydrogen production considering the low-cost and environmentally friendly composition of glucose. The C-dots based anode exhibits a very good stability at 0.6 V vs. RHE under AM 1.5 G irradiation. After 10 h illumination, the original J keeps 95% of its initial value, suggesting its excellent PEC durability [8]. This performance is similar to NiOOH/FeOOH/C-dots/BiVO4 [8], and much higher than C-dots/Cu2S (keeps 82% after 400 s illumination) [65], and C-dots/anatase-rutile TiO2 (keeps 92.3% after 5 h illumination) [44]. This stability is also much better compared to most of PEC devices based on inorganic QDs, which can keep less than 90% of its initial current density (Table 1) [17–19,25]. The calculated hydrogen production rate is ~36 mL cm−2·day−1, assuming the Faraday efficiency to be ~ 95% in a three-electrode system. Considering that TiO2, C-dots, glucose and Pt/CNFs are all nontoxic, cost-effective, and stable, the proposed ecofriendly PEC devices pave the way for further hydrogen production.

In order to gain further insight on the improved efficiency in R C-dots/TiO2 based anode, compared with the anode using carboxyl group capped C-dots, the charge transfer in the photoanodes and at the interface of the photoanode/electrolyte was evaluated by using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS, Fig. S12). The value of electron transfer resistance at the photoanode (Rct) and the photoanode/electrolyte interface (Rss) obtained from the R C-dots/TiO2-sensitized photoelectrode is the lowest compared to anodes based on carboxyl group capped C-dots/TiO2 and bare TiO2 (Table S2). This result indicates the decrease of interfacial charge transfer resistance at the photoanode, which contributes to the high current density in R C-dots based PEC devices. [70].

In summary, we designed and demonstrated an ecofriendly PEC device using R C-dots/TiO2 as an efficient and stable anode, glucose as electrolyte and Pt/CNFs as cost-effective and efficient counter electrode for hydrogen production. The C-dots prepared using a solvothermal reaction have an absorption spectrum ranging from 300 to 600 nm with a QY of 50%. Compared to carboxyl capped C-dots, the hydroxyl group on the C-dots can strongly interact with the TiO2, forming a very stable complex. Benefiting from these features, the R C-dots/TiO2 anode exhibits a saturated J as high as ~4 mA/cm2 at 0.6 V vs. RHE using glucose as hole scavenger and Pt (Pt/CNFs) as counter electrode. The J of the PEC device can maintain 95% of its initial value after 10 h illumination. This work indicates that the R C-dots/TiO2-Glucose system potentially applies to the design and development of ecofriendly PEC devices and other optoelectronic devices, suggesting a promising way for green hydrogen production through the use of eco-compatible processes and environmentally friendly resources.
